essay_text:
Since I was five, I've traveled alone every year to Ecuador to see my mother's side of the family. With my
grandparents, I learned Spanish with Ecuadorian rhythm and experienced Ecuadorian culture. During New
Year's, for example, we celebrate a tradition of making paper mache figures of old men and igniting them
as a way to, "Quemar el afio viejo." I valued being part of these traditions because it was the only time |
got exposure to these customs and was able to feel "truly" Ecuadorian.

I sometimes felt out of place, my cousins pointing out when I used improper Spanish and how I was not
used to walking miles from one shop to another in town. Still, these visits are irreplaceable because they
gave me an outlet to experience Ecuadorian culture and history.

To add to my cultural experiences, at age seven, I discovered another part of my identity: my Belizean
heritage. My father's side of the family lived in Belize, and he'd married and divorced prior to moving to
the US. As a result, I had three half-siblings including an engaged sister.

That spring I visited Belize where my sister was marrying, one brother was getting ready for college, and
my other brother was leaving to compete in international volleyball tournaments. Feeling part of my
father's side of the family was wonderful, yet difficult. Months before the trip I had begged him to teach me
Kriol to better understand people there, but he refused, calling it "broken English," saying I'd be looked
down upon at school for having bad speaking habits. So I went to Belize with almost no Kriol, and it was
frustrating and confusing and distressing. I felt disconnected. My family tried talking to me, but their Kriol
was too fast, and their Caribbean accents too heavy, so I just nodded and pretended I knew what they said
so the constant question, "What?" wouldn't get annoying.

Throughout middle school, I gradually recognized my unique identity within LatinX communities in Los
Angeles. At times this made me feel isolated from other Hispanics. My classmates called me uncivilized
when I talked about eating cuy in Ecuador as a traditional dish, weird for using Spanish vocabulary like
canguil instead of palomitas, and when acknowledging my nationality became too much work for
someone, "brown" became a sufficient enough label.

As a Latina in a predominantly Mexican part of Los Angeles, assumptions that I was Mexican frequently
resulted in awkward apologies for not relating to what I saw as a common culture everyone else seemed
to be a part of. For the longest time, I accepted I'd always be the only Ecuadorian or Belizean at my
school. At the same time, I questioned how much of me I considered Ecuadorian or Belizean or American.
 
How was I supposed to concisely identify as an Ecuadorian-Belizean-American?
Did my inability to speak Kriol make me less Belizean?

How many more times did I have to "prove" I was Ecuadorian?

It was through books that I finally came to terms with my identity and I could stop feeling so out of place
within my own cultures. "This Bridge Called My Back: Writings by Radical Women of Color" was
especially formative in my change. Its anthology of essays about cultural struggles made me feel more
accepted by my LatinX community. I still felt like my background was outside the norm, but rather than be
self-conscious, I decided to embrace it and share my story. I'm proud to be a Hispanic-Caribbean woman.
I'm proud of my multifaceted heritage. Throughout my life, I've had to balance multiple parts of my
identity, and at times it felt impossible to be more than "one-third" of myself, but now I see that my
identity was never meant to be split into three parts; it's a rich blend of multicolored paint, and I'm proud
to display my portrait.
prompt: Some students have a background, identity, interest, or talent that is so meaningful they believe their application would be incomplete without it. If this sounds like you, then please share your story.
persona_prompt: You are a writer who crafts introspective personal narratives, often reflecting on identity and cultural heritage. Weave together chronological accounts with moments of vulnerability and self-questioning. Use specific cultural details and rhetorical questions to engage the reader, culminating in a sense of hard-won self-acceptance and pride. Your voice is earnest and reflective, yet ultimately resilient.
sentence_style: Varied. The essay employs direct, clear narrative sentences for exposition, interspersed with shorter, impactful sentences to convey emotional states. Rhetorical questions are a key feature for expressing internal conflict and inviting reader contemplation. Longer, more complex sentences are used for deeper reflection and metaphorical conclusions.
vocabulary_level: Moderate — The writer uses precise emotional vocabulary and integrates specific cultural terms naturally without excessive explanation, assuming an engaged reader.
structural_patterns: The essay follows a chronological narrative structure, moving from childhood experiences to adolescent self-discovery. It uses personal anecdotes to illustrate broader themes of identity. The narrative progresses from initial confusion and alienation to a resolution of self-acceptance, employing a problem-solution arc.
distinctive_moves: Integrating specific cultural vocabulary (e.g., 'Quemar el afio viejo,' 'cuy,' 'canguil,' 'Kriol') to enrich the narrative.
Utilizing rhetorical questions to convey internal struggle and invite reader empathy.
Employing vivid, sensory details and emotional adjectives to describe experiences and feelings (e.g., 'frustrating and confusing and distressing').
Drawing a clear arc from feeling 'out of place' to embracing 'multifaceted heritage' with pride.
Concluding with a powerful metaphor to symbolize identity as a 'rich blend of multicolored paint'.
themes_and_preoccupations: Multifaceted cultural identity and heritage (Ecuadorian, Belizean, American, LatinX).
The search for belonging and the experience of alienation within and between cultures.
The role of language (Spanish, Kriol) in connection and disconnection.
The journey of self-discovery and acceptance in reconciling diverse parts of one's identity.
self_presentation: The writer presents as initially vulnerable and questioning, admitting to feelings of confusion, frustration, and isolation. This evolves into an analytical and ultimately confident, proud stance, demonstrating resilience and deep introspection.
cultural_or_contextual_markers: The essay is rich with markers of Ecuadorian traditions, Belizean heritage (especially Kriol language and Caribbean accents), and the specific social dynamics of LatinX communities in Los Angeles. It also references a formative literary work, 'This Bridge Called My Back'.
avoid_in_imitation: Overly academic or detached analytical language that lacks personal emotional connection.
A purely linear narrative without moments of introspection or self-doubt.
A preachy or didactic tone; maintain an earnest, reflective voice.
sample_sentence: Often, I found myself navigating unspoken cultural nuances, a quiet observer attempting to stitch together the disparate threads of my own evolving story.
